A first glance on the change from aiohttp 2.0.0 to 2.2.0:
The  session used in ``aiohttp.request`` is attached to the ``aiohttp.ClientResponse`` but not closed by anyone - only a direct access to the protected member ``aiohttp.ClientResponse._session.close()`` can close it.
Introduced in https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1985
This is causing the resource warning even on the closed response.

``release`` or ``close`` : I am not familiar with connection pooling, but ``release`` should be fine.
In addition we could use a ``aiohttp.ClientSession`` attached to the ``hangups.client.Client`` and use it for all the aiohttp requests.

Using ``aiohttp==2.2.0`` and hangups spamms every log atm.

I think we should mention that `to_dict` will be preferable over `__dict__` since there are a lot of third party classes that implements this (and sometimes depends on it). One example would be using an ORM and returning the user object, where `__dict__` is mostly likely to be available - and the result would not be, well, not "advisable" so to speak. Quick example using SQLAlchemy:

```python
class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'users'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String)
    fullname = Column(String)
    password = Column(String)


some_user = session.query(User).first()
print(hasattr(some_user, '__dict__'))
# >> True
print(dict(some_user))
# >> {'_sa_instance_state': <sqlalchemy.orm.state.InstanceState object at 0x7fb9de99f780>}
```

I'm not totally sure this is the correct way to handle repeated notes with sustain. The way we did it on magenta was to extend each note within a sustain to either the beginning of the next note of the same pitch or the end of the sustain period, whichever happens first:

https://github.com/tensorflow/magenta/blob/master/magenta/music/sequences_lib.py#L972

The pianoroll equivalent of that would be to just retain whatever the most recent velocity was, not the maximum. My argument for using the most recent velocity would be that if you play a note with high velocity on a piano with sustain on, then wait a long time, then play the same note with low velocity, you won't hear a loud note, you'll hear a soft note.

That said, I could see it going the other way too (play a soft note immediately after a loud note), but I think retaining the most recent velocity better captures what's going on without making too many assumptions about the acoustic properties of the instrument.

What do you think?

A noop is a version where we don't have any addons to install/upgrade and no operations → there is no point to run a backup for a noop version. We should not need to write 

```
- 10.1.0:
   backup: false
```

Only to disable the backup on a noop version. `is_noop` should not be changed then. However, when `is_noop` is True, the backup should not be performed.

Also, if we have 3 consecutive versions that needs a backup, we only need a backup before the first (take care of the case where this is a new database).

I see it this way:

In the runner (https://github.com/camptocamp/marabunta/blob/8b5192076079ce8d3ecacec043cc014902a75ce4/marabunta/runner.py#L79), before executing the versions, loop through all the versions and if at least one version needs a backup and the global backup option is configured, then run it.

The consumer-group can not exist for two reasons:
a) The consumer actually does not exist.
b) The consumer exists and is running but has never committed offsets. As a result kafka cannot find it.
We should modify the log accordingly to avoid confusion for the team.
This can be tested and confirmed by running a new-consumer and having offsets committed (auto_offset_reset=False and no manual commit). Ref.: https://yelpwiki.yelpcorp.com/display/DISTSYS/Yelp+Kafka

Also, I think it's offset_set command with --force option, where would want to return the empty result without exiting. The rationale behind this is that we can still commit offsets (set-offsets) even if consumer-group is not found. This is worth testing with above setup too.

I once thought the same as you, but I've learned a bit more about type theory since then. =) Specifically, the type of the callable is not just "callable", but `Union[(array -> float), (array -> array)]`. That is: the inputs and outputs of the callable are part of the type. (Whereas a ValueError would stem from, e.g. returning a float that is outside some allowed range, for example.)

And, as I mentioned, you should catch *TypeError*, because that is the error we are detecting: when the function is unhappy with the inputs as provided.

The errors will propagate through if they are of a different type, and this is what we want: our error message that "you have provided the wrong type of function" will not necessarily be accurate if we catch all errors. If someone, for example, provides a threshold function that sends the data to some Google Compute Engine to process on their TPUs (to get back a threshold 😂), and there is a NetworkError, we don't want to say that it's the wrong kind of function: it is the right kind, but it failed for extraneous reasons.

It's a little messy, but the L2 split point is definitely doable in O(N).  Here's some math (I haven't tried to code any of this up yet).  We have a bunch of point (xj,fj), and we want to find the point i where the error is minimized.  This is the sum of errors for 0<j<i and i<j<N  (where N is the last point, aka -1).

The linear approximations are:

g_left(x) = f0 + (fi-f0)/(xi-x0) * (x-x0)
g_right(x) = fN + (fi-fN)/(xi-xN) * (x-xN)

The left-side L2 error is:

err_left = sum_0<j<i (fj - gleft(xj))^2
    = sum_0<j<i (fj - f0)^2
       + 2(fi-f0)/(xi-x0) sum_0<j<i (fj-f0)(xj-x0)
       + (fi-f0)^2/(xi-x0)^2 sum_0<j<i (xj-x0)^2

The right-side error is the same, but sum_i<j<N and x0->xN, f0->fN.

These sums can all be efficiently computed with `numpy.cumsum`, so I think everything is O(N) and vectorizable, so it should be pretty fast.  Do you want to try coding it up?  Or would you like me to give it a go?

Right.  It's not arbitrary.  `ctypes.data` in python really is returning an integer, since python can't deal with any kind of pointer directly.  So the dance is that numpy's C-layer data object is really a `double*`.  Then in python you can access it as an integer and pass it to your C-layer functions.  Then you recast it back to a `double*`.  I use `size_t`, because that's the C++ name for the integer type that is guaranteed to hold the same number of bytes as a pointer, which makes it safe to do this.

For more information, [here](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.13.0/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.ctypes.html) is the numpy description of the ctypes.data attribute which says it is "A pointer to the memory area of the array as a Python integer."

Hi @tjprescott, when the user asks for restore disks, there are 2 pieces of important information which needs to be given back to the user.
1)	The configuration of the backed up VM (in the form of VMConfig.json)
2)	The data of the backed up VM i.e., disks of the VM.

We always need a storage account to push the configuration file. Previously both the artifacts were restored to the storage account specified by the user in the restore command. Now, with the new flag, users have the capability of deciding the destination of the restored disks. When this flag is set to true, the disks will be restored to the storage account provided in the command (via --storage-account). When the flag is set to false, the disks will be restored to the original storage accounts of the disks themselves.

    OUTPUT_VARIABLE _numpy_conv_template_file
    OUTPUT_STRIP_TRAILING_WHITESPACE

endif()

There's a `ConstructorStats.detail_reg_inst()` you can call here which handles the GC collection (including doing it twice for PyPy), and also returns the number of currently tracked instances.  Aside from not needing the manual GC calls at all (and not needing to worry about invoking it twice under PyPy), it might be a useful check here, as well, to make sure that another object hasn't been created/copied somewhere along the line:

```Python
    n_inst = ConstructorStats.detail_reg_inst()
    # ... create parent, child
    assert ConstructorStats.detail_reg_inst() == n_inst + 2
    # ... destroy
    assert ConstructorStats.detail_reg_inst() == n_inst
```

So we don't actually need `__all__` at all :-)

If you do `from module import *`, then Python:

1. Checks for an `__all__`. If it finds one, then that's what it uses.
2. Otherwise, it imports all names that are defined in the module, and don't start with an underscore.

So we can (and should) delete this `__all__` entirely. So long as we're careful about what names we export – which is something we need to do anyway – then it's totally redundant.
